does: This paper proposes preference embedding, a latent-space approach that represents responses as vectors to capture complex preference structures with linear query complexity, and introduces General Preference Optimization (GPO), a generalized preference score-based algorithm extending reward-based RLHF. The authors present GPM (General Preference embedding Model) and demonstrate through RewardBench experiments that it substantially outperforms traditional Bradley-Terry reward models, particularly on intransitive and cyclic preferences where BT models fail. They validate downstream improvements on AlpacaEval2.0 and other tasks when using GPO with their general preference model during language model post-training, establishing empirical superiority over existing approaches.
connects: This work builds on decades of preference modeling research, extending beyond the Bradley-Terry framework that has dominated pairwise comparison modeling since its introduction. It responds to known limitations in traditional reward models used in modern RLHF pipelines, which assume transitivity and struggle with non-linear preference structures. The paper connects to recent work on reward model benchmarking (RewardBench) and contemporary RLHF methods like DPO and related approaches, while drawing on the preference learning literature's recognition that human preferences often violate transitivity assumptions. It addresses the explicit gap between classical preference theory and practical language model alignment, where human feedback exhibits complex patterns that simpler reward models cannot represent.
enables: This general preference modeling framework opens avenues for more sophisticated alignment methods that can handle realistic human preference structures, including cyclic and intransitive relationships. The linear complexity embedding approach enables scaling preference modeling to longer sequences and more complex reasoning tasks. Future work can build on GPO to develop improved human feedback collection strategies that leverage non-transitive preference discovery, design better downstream training objectives using richer preference structures, and extend the framework to multi-dimensional or contextual preferences. The work also enables more rigorous evaluation of preference models beyond standard benchmarks, potentially informing the design of better human feedback mechanisms for foundation model alignment at scale.
